entry.title: Re: How to configure a movement type relevant for MRP
entry.content:
What is the Business requirement?
First of all, you need to understand the MRP functionality. In short, from sales side, as and when sales order are created, in the backend, system calculates the required quantity for the saleable material. This calculation depends on total open sale orders less inventory on-hand. Of course, it also depends on the MRP views you set in the material master. If the required quantity is less, MRP run will generate requirements to meet the demand.
Having said that, not in all cases, movement type trigger the above process and it is different

entry.title: FAQ: How to Get the Unique Identifier (UUID) of Objects in SAP Field Service Management
entry.content:
I got asked today how to find the unique ID of an object in SAP Field Service Management — for example, an activity, equipment, or business partner.
At first, I thought I knew the answer. But as I started checking, I realized there are a few different ways to get the ID, depending on the object type — and even a few tricks I hadn’t used before.

Since it was a good reminder (and I learned something new myself), I thought I’d share it here in case it’s helpful for others too. Whether you’re troubleshooting, working with APIs, or just trying to understand where a certain ID comes from — this guide gives you a quick overview of your options.

🔧 1. Activities and Service Calls: Use the "Share" Action

For transactional objects like activities or service calls, the easiest way to get the unique ID (UUID) is to use the “Share” action in the UI.

How:

  • Open the activity or service call.

  • Click the Action (3 dot) icon on the top right and select Share.

  • The link is automatically copied to your clipboard.

  • The UUID is part of the URL, for example:

    https://<your-cluster>.fsm.cloud.sap/shell/#/planning-dispatching/activities/view/2EC9FC3630E54988AB7CAB98904A1222/details?selectedCompanyId=<your-company-id>

Get the activity id from the user interface

This UUID can be used directly in business rules, screen configurations, reports, API requests or integrations.

🧩 2. Master Data (e.g., Equipment, Business Partner): Opening the URL from Linked Transactions 

For master data objects like equipment or business partners the ID isn’t shown directly in the UI when opening it within the master data module of SAP Field Service Management — but there is a trick to get the URL including UUID:

Open the object from a Linked Object

  • Open an activity or service call that references the equipment or business partner.

  • Click on the linked object.

  • Check the browser URL — the UUID is part of it:

    https://<your-cluster>.fsm.cloud.sap/shell/#/master-data-management/businesspartner/details/058BAE32613E4EA6B6AF6539A601DA9A

This works well if you're already working inside a transaction and want to trace the related objects.

🔍 3. Use the SAP Field Service Management Query API to query by known values

If you know certain values like an external ID, code or name, you can also use the FSM Query API to look up the object and get its UUID.

Examples:

  • Equipment by Serial Number:

    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.serialNumber FROM Equipment e WHERE e.serialNumber = 'LV_OHL_FDR01_P23-P45'

  • Business Partner by code:

    SELECT bp.code, bp.id, bp.name FROM BusinessPartner bp WHERE bp.code = 'C0001'

The response will show the UUID in the id column. You can use the "Create Query" screen in the Analytics and Reporting or Admin Portal to run these queries.

💡 Tip: This method is especially useful when you need to look up multiple UUIDs or when working with integration scenarios.

Get any ID using Query in Analytics and Reporting

 

📎 Related Use Cases

  • Building custom reports, business rules, screen configurations or dashboards

  • Automating data flows between systems

  • Debugging or investigating sync issues

  • Creating cross-references between objects

✅ Summary

Object Type Method 1 Method 2
Activity / Service Call"Share" action in UIQuery API
Equipment / Business PartnerLinked object → URLQuery API
 

Let me know if you'd like a follow-up post with more tips and tricks and answers to frequently asked questions — or if you’ve found other useful tricks for working with FSM IDs!

SAP Field Service Management

entry.title: Re: How do I return an entity if it already exists?
entry.content:
Hi there,

from my understanding you are going against the odata standard with your current approach. What you are looking for is an UPSERT basically, in OData, this is done via a PUT or PATCH (https://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/odata/v4.01/odata-v4.01-part1-protocol.html#sec_UpsertanEntity). Depending on your exact use case you want to use PUT or PATCH in CAP. Also for newer version you may have to enable a certain flag: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/may25#revised-handling-of-put-requests

 

So if you try sending a PATCH request to a not existing entity, the CAP Framework will create said entity then. If it already exists, then it will update it.

For you case the issue you have now is with your primary key. You must know the primary key in order to send a PATCH request. If you use cuid, then you will have a UUID ID field that is the primary key. Your client very likely can't know this ID without asking the backend first, which won't be possible if you deactive bulk read. So an option would be to change the primary key. Currently you are treating the url property as a key anyway, so it might be worth considering to use the URL itself as the key. If you choose the URL as the primary key you could send a PATCH request like this:

PATCH  <your server>/<your service>/Links('https://dummy.sap')
{ }

This would create a Links entity with that url if it doesn't exist, otherwise it should update it (and also update the managed fields)

Hope that helps and cheers
Marco

entry.title: Re: How to filter and analyze based on Ship-To Party’s Ci...
entry.content:
Hello @Geetank_Vats89 

Thank you for raising the requirement to capture the City/District of the Ship-To Party at the item level in the Sales Order for reporting purposes.

Based on the current capabilities of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the recommended solution would be,

Custom Field Creation,
We can create a custom field at the Sales Order Item level using the Custom Fields and Logic app. This allows us to store the City/District value directly on each item.

Automated Population via BADI,
Using the BADI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM, we can implement logic to fetch the City/District of the Ship-To Party from the partner function or business partner master data and automatically populate the custom field during Sales Order processing.

Reporting Enablement,
Once the field is populated, we can extend standard reports like List Sales Orders, making the City/District available for filtering and analysis.

This approach follows SAP’s best practices for extensibility in Public Cloud, ensuring the solution remains compliant, upgrade-stable, and fully supported.

With kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: Random error Cannot convert data <.> into type <DECIMAL>. Context: Column <>
entry.content:
We are using SAP BODS on  an Oracle database. From time to time the same dataflow aborts with the error Cannot convert data <.> into type <DECIMAL>. Context: Column <>  . Without specification which data or column is affected.

All mappings on decimal fields  in this dataflow are from decimal to decimal or from decimal to integer. All decimals are of the form decimal(n,0) .  The underlying Oracle columns are number(38,0). The same dataflow is successful when executed  a bit later but on presumably the same data -no transformations are run on this data between abortion and new run.  The error message provides the name of  the transform which fails and references some Join48 which is probably  some execution step of the transform. But we are not able to exactly localize the causing issue.  Is there a way to find the  information, what object the Join48 is referring to?

entry.title: Re: Mass Update Credit Limit Issue
entry.content: It is not possible to mass update the credit limit of business partners with Basic Credit Management scope. Scope item 1QM (Advanced Credit Management) needs to be activated for the options to mass update the credit limit to be available. This scope item requires additional SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition license.

entry.title: Re: Sales &and Profitability IFP Calculating Sales Deduct...
entry.content:
Hi @SANA11

In cases like rebates, where the value varies based on both the customer and the product, it's not possible to calculate the rebate amount using dimension properties alone.

Instead, the rebate ratio must be stored as fact data in the model, and the rebate amount should be calculated based on that.

  • Dimensions & Measrues

-The Measure Rebate & Rebate Ratio are added.

-Channel Dimension is added. 

 

  • Rebate Ratio Data

-The rebate ratio data must be assigned to specific members for all dimensions except the product and customer dimensions (e.g., Entity: Unassigned,   Channel: Unassigned)

 

  • Data action script
MEMBERSET [d/Date] = "202507"
MEMBERSET [d/Entity] = "US"

DATA([d/Measures]="Rebate") = RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures]="Gross_Revenue") 
                            * RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures]="Rebate_Ratio",[d/Entity]="#",[d/Channel]="#")
  • Story: Source Data

 

  • Story: Calculated result

 

I hope this is helpful to you, and if I have misunderstood anything, please feel free to reach out.

Moonjun

entry.title: How to Upload .mtaext File to a Node in SAP Cloud Transport Management via API?
entry.content:
Hi Community,

I'm working with SAP Cloud Transport Management (TMS) and looking to upload .mtaext files to specific nodes (e.g., QA, PROD) after the corresponding .mtar file has been uploaded and transported.

While exploring the Project "Piper" source code, I came across a method UploadMtaExtDescriptorToNode, which sends a POST request to the following endpoint:

/v2/nodes/{nodeId}/mtaExtDescriptors

It posts a .mtaext file via multipart/form-data, along with mtaVersion and description fields. I tried using this endpoint directly via Postman, but it resulted in an exception (500 Internal Server Error). I also noticed that this endpoint is not documented in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, which makes it unclear whether this API is supported or deprecated.

My question:

Is there any recommended way to upload .mtaext files via API after .mtar has been uploaded and transported?

Any guidance would be appreciated. Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Develop Powerful Analytical Queries using ABAP Core Data Services (CDS)
entry.content:
Hello Alberto,

your observation is correct, that display-attributes and also texts (for dimensions and display attributes) are read by the analytical engine in a different step and the "join" is done in the application server.

"Pushdown" only influences the way how cube data is processed. With "pushdown" e.g. exception aggregation is handled by the HANA. Without "pushdown" the analytical engine will add an additional "group-by" to the statement and calculate the exception aggregation in the application server. Usually "with pushdown" is faster but there are situations where the handling in the application server is faster.

This (reading cube-data and attributes and text separately) is standard behavior of the analytical engine and not specific to CDS. 

If you have problems with the performance of reading attributes, you should open an incident. Then some experts will analyze and check if it can be optimized.

Regards,

Stefan

entry.title: Gamification Workshop: Business Process Management Meets Change Management
entry.content:
How can organizations better embed Business Process Management (BPM) in daily work? And how can gamification help make process thinking more accessible—and even fun? These were the questions we explored in our recent workshop at the SAP AppHaus in Heidelberg.

Bringing together 16 participants from 9 organizations, the event provided an interactive space for learning, exchanging ideas, and developing new perspectives on BPM and change management.

A Day of Insights, Dialogue & Practice

The morning combined theoretical input with hands-on exercises to bring process challenges to life. One of the highlights was the Slotter Game—a fun and surprisingly effective way to experience the importance of coordination and process clarity. 

⚙️ Slotter Game – Making Process Thinking Tangible

In this team activity, two players stood on opposite sides of a gear-based setup, each tasked with guiding a coin through the rotating mechanism. The challenge? They could only see their own side of the board—unable to view the gear layout or the other player’s coin—often leading them to unintentionally block each other’s paths.

The game was played in three rounds:

  • Round 1 – No communication: Players acted independently. The process was slow and uncoordinated.
  • Round 2 – Free communication: Talking helped—but without structure, friction remained.
  • Round 3 – With a process diagram: A shared visual guide allowed smooth, fast coordination—proving the power of clear process documentation.

This simple exercise demonstrated how transparency, structure, and collaboration can drastically improve outcomes.

🔍 Real-World Challenges from Everyday BPM

In the afternoon, participants tackled real challenges submitted in advance. Discussions focused on three key areas:

  • Change & Adoption: BPM is often seen as abstract. Without visible benefits or clear stories, engagement remains low.
  • Roles & Governance: Unclear responsibilities and the perception of BPM as extra work limit impact.
  • Silos & Collaboration: A lack of end-to-end ownership and disconnected teams lead to inefficiency and friction.

The teams generated a wide range of creative solutions—many of which could be applied directly within their own organizations:

  • Lighthouse Process: Start with one well-chosen, visible process where BPM works really well—ideally one with measurable results and broad relevance. Showcase it as a "lighthouse" to demonstrate what’s possible. This creates internal momentum, builds trust, and motivates others to follow.
  • Internal Process Podcast: Create a short, regular audio format where employees share stories about process improvements, lessons learned, or successful change initiatives. Hearing familiar voices and practical examples builds relevance and lowers barriers to engagement
  • Ideas Management Program: Empower employees to contribute their own improvement ideas. Provide a clear submission path, give feedback, and reward valuable contributions (with visibility, points, or even small prizes). This encourages ownership and makes BPM a shared responsibility rather than a top-down directive.
  • BPM Community Event: Host informal internal events—virtual or in-person—where teams can explore BPM topics together. Think “process fair” with live demos, success story booths, games, and tool tips. These events build a sense of community and position BPM as something people can actively shape.
  • Gamified Adoption: Leverage holidays and add playful elements to process models—for example, hiding a Santa Claus or Easter Bunny icon in diagrams and rewarding those who find them all. It’s a lighthearted way to encourage exploration and familiarity with tools like Signavio, especially for new users.

A big thank-you to all attendees for their energy and openness. The workshop clearly showed that BPM can be interactive, engaging, and even fun—with the right formats and a collaborative mindset.

💬 What about you?

What creative formats or gamified approaches have you seen work in BPM and change initiatives?
We’d love to hear your ideas—share them with the Signavio Community!

entry.title: SAP PO Output Form – Requirement to Consolidate Line Items for Same Material in SAP Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAPians and Adobe Form Developers,

In SAP Public Cloud, while creating a Purchase Order, I am required to use the same material with different WBS elements as per our business requirements. For example, we may have a single material assigned to 10 different WBS elements, resulting in 10 separate line items in the PO — each with the same material code but a different WBS.

This structure works for internal tracking purposes. However, when we share the PO output form with the vendor, we need to present it as a single line item with the cumulative quantity, since from the vendor’s perspective it is a single material without the need to differentiate by WBS.

In the standard SAP Adobe Form, the output currently displays all 10 line items, matching the PO structure in the system.

Could you please advise how we can achieve the following in the Adobe Form output:

  • Display the same material as a single consolidated line item

  • Show total cumulative quantity

  • Retain internal WBS assignment only within the system (not shown in vendor form)

Looking forward to your guidance on how this can be handled through Adobe Form customization

entry.title: Using Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP Applications (Db2 12.1 and Higher)
entry.content:
During numerous customer engagements, I learned that there is an increasing need, or even a mandate, to enable database auditing due to company policies, industry regulations, or the law. Auditing typically is enabled at both the SAP application level as well as the database level. Database-level auditing additionally captures workload and connections originating from outside the core SAP application, like direct connections to the database from a command line interface or the workload of third-party tools.
However, auditing both the SAP application and the database can lead to significant redundancy in audit data and thus can increase storage requirements.
This raises an important question: How can this redundancy be minimized or avoided, while still maintaining a secure and compliant audit trail?

 

Background

The basic concept of auditing in Db2 for Linux, UNIX, and Windows has already been described in the blog post Using db2audit with SAP Applications. In addition, that blog post explains how to address the performance impact of auditing and how to reduce the amount of database-level audit data with Db2 11.5 and earlier.
However, the methods described still generate a considerable volume of audit data, so you may ask whether there is an additional measure for reducing the amount of database audit data collection.

Answer: Yes, there is.

Using the functionality of Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications can significantly reduce the amount of database audit data. Starting with Db2 12.1, the workload of the SAP NetWeaver Application Server ABAP primary connection for the SAP database connect user can be excluded from database audit. Consequently, the generation of redundant audit data can be significantly reduced, too. Technically, this is based on an audit exception for a trusted context, using a trust procedure. In addition, the Db2 database server processes an application token in combination with a NONCE to securely identify the SAP application.

Too complex? No, don’t worry.

At first glance, the concept consisting of a trusted connection, a trust procedure, and an audit exception sounds complex to set up, but fortunately it is not. You do not need to understand the implementation details of the trust procedure, application token, or NONCE, as all of this is already available and pre-defined in Db2 for SAP environments.
In this blog post, I describe the setup of filtered audit step-by-step. Additionally, I conducted a series of tests to explore how much the audit data volume gets reduced and whether there also exists a performance benefit with using Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications.

 

Prepare your system

To take advantage of the filtered audit functionality, your system must meet several prerequisites. The required features are available by default as of Db2 Version 12.1 for Linux, UNIX, and Windows.

  • If your environment is still running on Db2 11.5 or lower, an upgrade is required. For information about the upgrade process, see Upgrading to Version 12.1 of IBM Db2 for Linux, UNIX, and Windows on SAP Help Portal.
  • In addition, you need to update the SAP NetWeaver Application Server ABAP kernel as described in SAP Note 3422696 - DB6: enable trusted context for reduced application auditing.
  • Finally, there are new security screens in the DBA Cockpit that help you check and monitor Db2 audit. The blog post DBA Cockpit Enhancements for Db2 12.1 describes the new functionality. The new screens are delivered as part of the DBA Cockpit related code corrections for Db2 12.1 as described in SAP Note 3532234 - DB6: DBA Cockpit for IBM Db2 for LUW 12.1: Requirements and Enhancements . Make sure to implement those code corrections in your system.

 

 

Get Started

Once your system is prepared and running on Db2 12.1, you can begin configuring the filtered audit functionality. This process is outlined in the chapter “Db2 Audit and Audit Exceptions” of the Database Administration Guide for SAP on IBM Db2 for Linux, UNIX, and Windows.

 The setup involves four key steps:

  1. Define a trusted context using a trust procedure.
  2. Establish a trusted connection.
  3. Define a database audit policy and activate it.
  4. Create an audit exception for the trusted context.

Trusted Context

The first step in configuring filtered audit is to define a trusted context.  Let’s first have a look at the new security screen in the DBA Cockpit (SAP transaction DBACOCKPIT). In the main menu, navigate to Configuration --> Security.

You should see entries already listed in the Trusted Context tab, which means the trusted context setup is already done. But how did this happen? Well, with Db2 V12.1 and higher, the trusted context setup typically happens automatically during the database installation post-processing or upgrade post-processing for SAP systems. Specifically, the db6_update_db script creates a trusted context for the SAP system database connect user (which is named “sapsr3” in my case).

Here’s an example of the SQL statement executed by the db6_update_db script:

CREATE TRUSTED CONTEXT DB6CTX_SAPSR3 BASED UPON CONNECTION USING SYSTEM AUTHID SAPSR3 ENABLE ATTRIBUTES (TRUST PROCEDURE SAPTOOLS.TRUST_PROC)

The above statement creates a trusted context named DB6CTX_SAPSR3, using a trust procedure called TRUST_PROC, which already exists in the SAPTOOLS schema. This procedure is specifically designed to work with SAP NetWeaver AS ABAP systems and allows for trusted connections to be established.

In the DBA Cockpit, you may also notice a second entry with the attribute “ENCRYPTION” set to “NONE”. This is automatically created with the trusted context and indicates that the context is valid for both encrypted and unencrypted connections. Check the CREATE TRUSTED CONTEXT statement in the IBM Db2 documentation for more details.

 

Trusted Connection

After having checked that the trusted context has already been set up automatically, let’s move to the Connections tab on the Security screen of the DBA Cockpit.

Most of the entries show disp+work processes of the SAP system, authenticated as SAP database connect user (which is “sapsr3” in the above example). The connections show “IMPLICIT” in the field “Trusted Context Type” and display the name of the already existing trusted context in the field “Trusted Context Name”. This indicates that the respective connections are trusted and are therefore subject to an audit exception.

You may also notice two entries in the list that do not display trusted context information.

  • One entry refers to an application named “db2bp”, indicating a database connection from a Db2 command line interface. These types of connections are intended to be audited and therefore are not implicitly trusted.
  • The second non-trusted entry represents a disp+work process. But in contrast to the other disp+work connections, it is authorized as the Db2 instance owner and represents a database connection initiated by the DBA Cockpit. Since the DBA Cockpit allows administrative database operations, auditing these connections is intended and they are therefore not implicitly trusted.   

 

Key takeaway

With the trusted context enabled, only the primary SAP application connections, established by the SAP connect user via a disp+work process, will be excluded from auditing.

  • Any other authorization ID that connects to the database
  • SAP executables like R3trans, R3load, db6util, tp, etc.
  • External tools like replication tools, monitoring tools, reporting tools, scheduling tools, etc.

 

Define and activate an audit policy

The most challenging step in the setup is probably the creation of an audit policy. While the technical setup is straightforward, the real challenge lies in defining the scope of what should be audited. Audit requirements are typically shaped by a combination of legislation, industry regulation, and company policies.  As a result, the audit policy must be tailored to each organization’s unique compliance landscape, and therefore I cannot provide a one-size-fits-all policy in this blog post.

And the good news is…

Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications reduces the amount of database audit data significantly. This makes it easier to define the scope of the database-level audit because it is now possible to implement a quite comprehensive audit policy while at the same time having a manageable audit trail and a significantly reduced volume of audit data.  In the examples shown in this blog post, I used such a comprehensive audit policy.

 

Process

To enable database auditing, you need to take the following steps:

  1. Define and create an audit policy.
  2. Configure target paths to store audit data and archived audit data.
  3. Configure the Db2 audit buffer.
  4. Activate the audit policy for your database.

The following example statement uses all available database audit categories to create a comprehensive audit policy. 

db2 "create audit policy comprehensivepolicy categories 
     audit status both, checking status both, 
     objmaint status both, secmaint status both, 
     sysadmin status both, validate status both, 
     execute status both, 
     context status both error type audit"

Next, configure two storage locations for the audit data files: one for active audit logs and another one for archived audit logs. Use the following commands to set these paths:

db2audit configure datapath /db2audit/auditlogs
db2audit configure archivepath /db2audit/auditarchive

 

Now configure the audit buffers. There exist two audit buffers and you can define their size using the database manager parameter “AUDIT_BUF_SZ”, in a unit of 4k pages. 
The default setting of the AUDIT_BUF_SZ parameter is “0”. This means that no buffers are used and that audit data will be written to disk synchronously. You may experience a significant performance impact when using this setting.
Make sure to set the AUDIT_BUF_SZ parameter to a value greater than “0”. In this case, two equally sized buffers will be allocated from the database heap. The setting of the parameter defines the size of each audit buffer. Using a round-robin approach, audit data is first written to one of the buffers. As soon as this first buffer gets full, or at certain time intervals, the content from the first buffer is flushed into an audit file on disk. At the same time, the database continues to write its audit data into the second audit buffer.
Tip: Check SAP Note 1474404 - DB6: Poor database performance with DB2AUDIT for a recommended setting of the audit buffer size. The value of 4096 4k-pages is a good starter.
Use the following command to set both audit buffers to a size of 4096 4k-pages each:

db2 "update DBM CFG using AUDIT_BUF_SZ 4096"

Finally, activate the policy as follows, using the name of the policy you defined when creating it:

db2 "audit database using policy comprehensivepolicy"

If necessary, you can deactivate the policy again by removing it, using the following command. This effectively will stop database-level auditing.

db2 "audit database remove policy"

On the Security screen of the DBA Cockpit, on the Db2 Audit tab, you can review your setup.

At the top of the screen, you can see the settings for the configured audit buffer size as well as the audit data and audit archive paths.
Note: Instance Audit active should be set to “No” as we configured database-level auditing only and no instance-level auditing.
The database-level audit policy settings are shown in the table on the screen. The second column Audit Status shows whether the configured policy is active or not, and the subsequent columns show the configuration of the individual audit categories.

 

Create an audit exception

In the last step of setting up Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications, you define an audit exception for the trusted context. With this, all activities of trusted connections are exempted from the database audit trail.
Use the following command to set up the audit exception for the trusted context:

db2 "audit add exception for trusted context DB6CTX_SAPSR3"

Next, switch back to the Trusted Context tab on the Security screen of the DBA Cockpit.

You now see the entry Audit Exception in the column Exception Type.
This means that you have successfully enabled Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications.

 

Testing Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP Applications

How effective is the filtered audit functionality?

To answer this question, I conducted a series of tests and measured both the volume of audit data as well as the performance impact. For all tests, Db2 database-level auditing was active using the same set of audit categories, that is, I used the comprehensive audit policy as described in this blog post. I executed the tests both with an active audit exception as well as without an audit exception, this means with and without using the Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications. The tests were performed on an SAP NetWeaver 7.50 AS ABAP system running on Db2 12.1 on Linux.

Test scenarios

I set up the following three test scenarios:

  1. Generating workload using the SAP HA Test Tool. This tool initially was created to test high-availability scenarios under workload. However, also for non-HA setups, it is perfectly suited for workload generation with a configurable load and runtime. In a nutshell, the tool processes order items, performing INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements on a handful of tables. The tool measures dialog steps per second as well as the response time.
  2. Re-generation of SAP ABAP programs with transaction SGEN and component SAP_ABA.
  3. Stopping and starting the SAP Application Servers, including the Db2 instance.

Before and after each test, I archived the Db2 audit log file to measure the amount of audit data generated during each test.
You can archive the active audit log using the following command:

db2audit archive database <DBSID>

 

Results

The following tables show the results of my tests using the comprehensive audit policy and an audit buffer of pages.

Looking at the results, the performance metrics are all in the same order of magnitude, with a slight but consistent advantage for the audit exception scenarios, which is expected with a properly tuned audit buffer.
The true benefit of Db2 Filtered Audit, however, is clearly visible when looking at the audit file sizes that represent the amount of audit data being generated during the tests.  The decrease of audit data varies between factor 8 for the starting and stopping SAP” test case, by factor 261 for the SAP HA test tool case, and by factor 2345 for the SGEN test case.

In a nutshell

Using Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications shows some performance benefits and reduces the amount of database audit data . Not only does a reduced volume of audit data save a significant amount of disk space, but it also eases the subsequent analysis of the audit data trail.

2nd test cycle

In a second test cycle, I evaluated the effect of the audit buffers on system performance. For this, I executed the SAP HA test tool multiple times, modifying the values of the database manager configuration parameter “AUDIT_BUF_SZ“ for each run.  I used audit buffer values of 0 (which results in a synchronous write to the audit file), 100, 1000, 4096, 10000 and 20000 pages.

First, let’s look at the results without using the audit exception for the trusted context.

With an audit buffer set to a value of 0, the measured number of dialog steps per second ended at 1,1 and the average response time at 78 seconds. You could call this being close to a virtual system standstill. Note the logarithmic scale on the response time axis in the chart. Even with a small audit buffer of 100 pages, the performance improved significantly and incrementally increased further with bigger audit buffer sizes.  The moderate value of 4096 pages of audit buffer, as recommended in SAP Note 1474404 - DB6: Poor database performance with DB2AUDIT, proved to be a good setting for this test case.

Next, let’s look at the results with using an audit exception for the trusted context.

With Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications in place, the performance penalty for synchronous writes of audit files was not as significant. Again, the SAP-recommended audit buffer size setting of 4096 pages proved to work very well.

 

Summary

Here are your key takeaways on using Db2 Filtered Audit for SAP applications:

  • It is straight-forward to set up, and monitoring is integrated in the DBA .
  • It reduces the amount of database audit data significantly and mainly avoids data redundancy with SAP application-based auditing.
  • It eases the definition of audit policies as it allows for more comprehensive policy setups while keeping the amount of generated audit data at a manageable level.
  • It still audits non-SAP workload as well as external SAP tools.
  • Having a properly sized audit buffer for good system performance is less important when using filtered audit. The SAP recommendation of 4096 4k pages is still a good starter.

entry.title: Driving Clean Core Adoption with SAP BTP: Highlights
entry.content:
Why Clean Core—and Why Now?

“Clean Core” is not another buzzword. It’s about making your SAP landscape future-proof by reducing technological "debt" of custom code and shifting to scalable, upgrade-safe extensions via SAP BTP.

 

Why it matters?

  • Quicker innovation cycles
  • Lower TCO over time
  • Simplified upgrades and landscape evolution
  • Readiness for AI, mobile, and cloud-native use cases

 

Agenda highlights & session recap

Welcome & Introduction - Speaker: Luis Felipe Lanz (SAP)

  • Set the stage with SAP’s strategic push on Clean Core
  • Call to action: Start with a use case, not a product

 [Link to presentation]

 

The Road to Clean Core: Why is it a Necessity? - Speaker: César Martín (SAP)

  • In-app, side-by-side, and developer extensibility options
  • SAP’s evolution from modification-heavy models
  • SAP BTP as the enabler of sustainable extensibility

 

SAP Cloud ALM Supporting Clean Core - Speaker: Prasanna Vedhartham (SAP)

  • Overview of Cloud ALM capabilities
  • Lifecycle orchestration as a driver of governance
  • Role of ALM in keeping extensions compliant and traceable

[Link to presentation]

 

SAP BTP AI Core and AI Capabilities - Speaker: Fernando Terry (SAP)

  • BTP’s foundation for AI consumption and integration
  • Clean Core as prerequisite for scalable AI use
  • Showcased AI scenarios already in production

[Link to presentation]

 

ABAP RAP (RESTful Application Programming Model) - Speaker: Daniel Álamo (SAP)

  • RAP for clean, upgrade-safe ABAP extensions
  • Strong focus on side-by-side clean development principles

[Link to presentation]

 

Success Story: A2K2C - Speaker: David Yáñez (SAP)

  • Journey from custom-heavy legacy to modular BTP setup
  • Using AI to help building the foundation of a Clean Core

[Link to presentation]

 

Success Story: Transformation lessons - Speakers: Maria Fernandez & Elton Machado (NTT DATA)

  • Clean Core implementation at scale across industries
  • Emphasis on managing change across large teams
  • BTP extensions in action and tools to analyze your custom code

 

Acceleration Packs & SAP Mobile - Speakers: Humberto Ordosgoitia & Ece Dagasan (SAP)

  • Overview of Mobile Start and prebuilt accelerators
  • Quick wins and live demo

[Link to presentation]

 

Hands-On Workshop (Creating apps with Joule) - Speaker: Daniel Álamo (SAP)

  • Live app creation using Joule
  • Demonstrated how BTP tools simplify clean extensions

 

Closing Notes / Adoption and Next Steps - Speaker: Oswaldo Seara (SAP)

  • How to engage with your BTP CSM and move forward
  • SAP initiatives to support the Clean Core and how can we help you to achieve it

[Link to presentation]

 

Key Takeaways

  • Clean Core is a strategic advantage, not a restriction

  • SAP BTP is the execution layer for extensibility, AI, and integration
  • Workshops show it’s not just theory—you can build now

 

What’s Next?

Here’s how to keep the momentum going:

  • 💬 Contact your SAP BTP Customer Success Manager
  • 🎓 Visit SAP Discovery Center Missions on BTP

entry.title: Custom tokens in Onboarding
entry.content:
Hi All,

I have the requirement to create custom tokens for Onboarding e-mails for various job relationships. I tried to create them via direct mapping and rules, however the tokens don't show the correct data. Multiple roles will be maintained for each new joiner, but the tokens always pick up the name of the role which is added first.

There is no way to link the name which should occur through the token to a particular job relationship. Only option I see for direct mapping or a business rule is to fetch the job relationship or its ID, which is not something I am looking for.

If anyone could share the solution, I would very much appreciate 🙂

Regards,

Piotr Retecki

entry.title: Re: EBS configuration problem
entry.content:
yes that is possible (within the search string usage customising where you currently have 000). But if algorithm 001 doesn't work, you can also keep 000.

But please check the following, because 001 must work in your case:

  1. The invoice has not yet been cleared in an earlier test
  2. Posting rule setup according to my first comment.
  3. External transaction assigned to the posting rule AND algorithm 001
  4. No search string activated.
  5. During import (FF_5 or other transaction) the BELNR number range must be 0 - 9999999999 (XBLNR is not relevant in your case)

Best Regards,
Henk Hoogerbrug

entry.title: Re: SAP RAP Draft identification
entry.content:
You can add the following annotation in the top of the consumption view

@ObjectModel.semanticKey: [ '<KeyField>' ]

entry.title: Ювілей SAP Ukraine
entry.content:
🎉SAP Україна 30 років!

У 2025 році світовий лідер у сфері корпоративних додатків і генеративного ШІ SAP відзначає три десятиліття роботи на українському ринку. З 1995 року компанія є надійним технологічним партнером для бізнесу та держави, допомагаючи впроваджувати інновації та сучасні ІТ-рішення.

Лідерство на ринку. Згідно з рейтингами Forbes Україна, SAP — технологічна платформа вибору для українського бізнесу:

  • 7 з 10 провідних приватних компаній
  • 14 з 20 найкращих роботодавців країни
  • Більшість ключових інвесторів обирають SAP

Розвиток і локалізація. Від реєстрації представництва у 1995 році до повної локалізації — SAP адаптувала ключові продукти для українського ринку. Українська мова офіційно додана до стандартного інтерфейсу рішень. Хмарне портфоліо охоплює понад 100 рішень для 26 галузей та 12 напрямків діяльності. З 2022 року SAP Ukraine — резидент Дія.City.

Потужна екосистема.

  • 65 фахівців у локальному офісі
  • 18 партнерських компаній
  • Співпраця з AHK Ukraine, ЄБА, Lviv IT Cluster
  • Університетський Альянс із 13 ЗВО

Підтримка України. За 2022-2024 роки SAP надала українським клієнтам понад 65 млн євро допомоги у вигляді безкоштовних послуг. Компанія активно підтримує цифровізацію Міноборони, критичну інфраструктуру та медичні закупівлі. Українські експортери отримали безкоштовний доступ до SAP Business Network.

Соціальна відповідальність. SAP підтримує освітні та благодійні ініціативи: Social Impact Award Ukraine, центр "Superhumans", фонд "Діти Героїв", "UAnimals", програму "Дівчата STEM" та багато інших проєктів.

Загалом від початку повномасштабного вторгнення SAP виділила понад 4,5 млн євро на благодійність.

"30 років для SAP в Україні — це історія незламної підтримки, інновацій і зростання. Разом із нашими клієнтами, партнерами та командою ми не тільки трансформуємо бізнеси, а й активно сприяємо розвитку і процвітанню країни. Наше зобов’язання — продовжувати сприяти цифровізації української економіки, а також підтримувати суспільство на шляху до відновлення та процвітаючого майбутнього." — Ганна Мікулицька, керівна директорка SAP Ukraine.

#SAPUkraine30 #DigitalTransformation #SAPCommunityKyiv #SAP

entry.title: Re: Equipment Long Text in different languages
entry.content:
Hello,

Could you please check whether the user has maintained the long text for the storage bin in both languages?
As per standard SAP behavior, if the long text is maintained only in English (EN), it will be visible when the user logs in with the EN language setting. However, if the user logs in with German (DE) and the long text is not maintained in DE, the field will appear blank. The long text will only be displayed if it is maintained in the respective logon language.

Regrads,
Neeraj

entry.title: Profit center is not being derived from 1st line item in SAP S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
While posting the entries, if the user is entering the profit center in 1st line item, then the system should derive the profit center in 2nd line item automatically, because document splitting is active here.


But the system is not deriving the profit center in 2nd line item automatically.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Profit center is not being derived from 1st line item...
entry.content:
Document splitting does not derive the account assignments on the entry view of the document, the exception to this is so called follow-up costs as realized exchange rate differences, cash discount lines and penalties which are split already in the entry view (not only in the entry view).

See help.sap.com documentation Document Splitting Process
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/7575d8ce581046a0a058a6ab408bdb50.html?locale=en-US

Here you see in the examples shown that the document splitting process derives the account assignment on the ledger view

See also Examples of Business Transactions Used in Document Splitting
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/dee14b1a6c264e46b78d22d96a36a8bc.html?locale=en-US

Unspecified Posting (Business Transaction 0000)
When you make unspecified postings in document splitting, business transaction 0000 is used.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/7ff994cc93c34eabadc118e593ae095f.html?locale=en-US
The following example represents a transfer posting between two profit and loss accounts. Both items are completely assigned to different profit centers, which means that no document splitting takes place. However, to ensure a zero-balance on the assigned profit centers, additional zero-balance clearing line items are created.

I reference you also for your information 

1628607 Document splitting: Business process assigned in entry view
As referenced therein 
In document splitting, you expect that certain business processes already contain all necessary account assignments and are no longer split because an account assignment according to cause is not recognizable from the document to be posted. This SAP Note describes how to handle these processes in configuration.

I reference you also for your information 
2801589 - Document type to derive account assignment into Balance sheet from P&L Account

entry.title: Re: S/4 Hana conversion to margin analysis
entry.content:
Hello

I'm sure you can find the process easily, but here are some practical notes for you based on my experience:

Account Base is more simple actually. the Goal for account base is to present exact P&L amount in FI 

But in Costing Base, your can evaluate some data differently 

the most important part , is to make şure that all accounting documents relate to a CO object or PA segment 

for primary cost elements , The relation to PA is Cost center, and you will transfer data to PA by PA assessment cycles 

However, for revenue element  during data entry you should field PA segment data 

ın okb9 you can make revenu element mandetory for PA segment :

if you are using Cost component structure and you want to spilit you CGS to Cost component, in costing base we use KE27 , but here the customizing is a little different :

SPRO=> Financial Accounting=>General Ledger Accounting=>Periodic Processing=>Integration=>Materials Management=>Define Accounts for Splitting the Cost of Goods Sold

Best regards

Ghazal Afshar

entry.title: Re: Customers Payment API https://api.sap.com/api/ccpcust...
entry.content:
I appreciate your inquiry regarding the APIs available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for posting and clearing payments. 

Here are the APIs that you can utilize:

- Journal Entry - Post (Synchronous): [Link](https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/f5c8d0579212c525e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US)
- Journal Entry - Post (Asynchronous): [Link](https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/22a267e571e948499fda007a65b27c64.html?locale=en-US)
- Journal Entry – Clearing (Asynchronous): [Link](https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/5142cdd767b04122a8beb6ecd460a922.html?locale=en-US)

Additionally, you can explore more APIs related to Finance in the SAP Help Portal Documentation: [APIs in Finance](https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/5792333ddf3c47eaad4314b071dfd684.html?locale=en-US).

https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-digital-manufacturing/operations-guide/role-templates?locale=en-US&q=DMCi_SAC_READ_ONLY

The DMCi_SAC_READ_ONLY role template overrides the DMCI_SAC_USER role template or any access levels assigned to an user in embedded SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: Re: [CPI] HTTP "Don't Throw Exception on Failure" doesn't...
entry.content:
Hi Palmer,

Thank you for your attention.

Note that in my example, I performed a controlled test, where the two messages are exactly the same, alternating only the HTTP Code between 400 and 500.
In CPI I receive the response, and just convert it to XML, in the same way in both scenarios.

For HTTP 500, I receive the payload on the ERP proxy without any problems. However, for 400, I receive the error.
It seems to me that the XI adapter performs different treatments depending on the HTTP Code.
I have tried to manipulate the headers, changing the HTTP Code, but the behavior is the same.
The only way I have found at the moment to successfully deliver the message to the proxy was to make a dummy HTTP request after the original, to obtain an HTTP Code 200, and then recover the original payload and deliver it to the ERP. I wouldn't want to have to do that.


Regards,
Miguel Motta

entry.title: IRN for tax credit transfer invoice posted via J1IG_ISDN
entry.content:
GST India - ISD solution

Dear Team,

Recently SAP has released lot of notes to support the new legal requirement of INput Service Distributor(ISD) in India. Towards this, we create Customer Invoice & Vendor invoice for transferring the Tax credit from Non ISD business Place to ISD business Place using tcode J1IG_ISDN.

link to SAP GA: https://ga.support.sap.com/dtp/viewer/#/tree/2259/actions/29120

Creating customer invoice, SAP has included tcode FB70 in J1IG_ISDN itself. This will ease the user to create tax credit transfer invoice with the single screen. 

My questions here is , it is also necessary to create IRN and QR code for this tax credit transfer invoice.  Since we dont have HSN , Unit of measurements etc., field available in FB70 via J1IG_ISDN, how IRN can be incorporated in this ISD invoice? 

Any one come across with this situation and any suggestions to solve this issue. I dont see any notes from SAP on this yet.

Best Regards,

Muniraja

entry.title: EPC-992 - WBS on consignment stock - Public cloud
entry.content:
We have a business case for assigning consignment stock to a WBS element in our public cloud solution.  We see from the following KBA’s that this is a solution that SAP has provided a solution for, but this does not seem to be available for Public Cloud. 

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003332652
https://me.sap.com/notes/0003033182

 

and these 2 which are for public cloud, but do not completely fit the issue

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003515778
https://me.sap.com/notes/0003476355 

 

 

The reasons for this requirement are:

Allowing WBS assignment directly in the Consignment Issue sales order would:
• Eliminate manual ledger adjustments
• Provide real-time profitability tracking at the production line level
• Improve reporting accuracy and efficiency
• Fulfill contractual obligations with customers regarding cost
transparency per production line

entry.title: Re: Changing default dropdown value in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
What you see in the dropdown fields is the value that is assigned to a specific key in the key/value table that serves as the basis for your dropdown. If you want to see the technical key for your 'not defined' value, go to 'personalization' on the top right of your WebUI screen and check the box 'display keys in dropdowns'. I am assuming your respective keys are initial.

The dropdowns are defined in the context node classes within your WebUI component. You should do the following:

  • Find out which component you have to adapt: click and hold some editable part of your component in WebUI, then press F2
  • Open your component in BSP_WD_CMPWB. If it is not enhanced yet, you have to enhance it.
  • Go to your search context node and look for the GET_V_ methods of your search fields. You will see how a table is passed to the value help descriptor, this is the table you need to change.
  • If this doesn't help, debug the GET_ method to check which value is displayed
